Chapter 4
Chirality

Section 4.1
INTRODUCTION TO CHIRALITY: ENANTIOMERS

Levels of Organic Structure
Organic compounds can be described using different levels of structure
Composition: molecular formula
Constitution: how atoms are connected; bonding patterns
Configuration: the three-dimensional positions of atoms
Conformation: positions of groups with respect to bond rotations and other “easy” processes
Each level is associated with a type of isomerism
Configurational isomers differ in configuration but are identical at higher levels

Chirality
A chiral molecule is not identical to its mirror image
Viz. , a chiral molecule is not superimposable on its mirror image in three dimensions
A molecule that is superimposable on its mirror image is called achiral
Chiral mirror images exhibit an interesting kind of configurational isomerism

Chirality & Enantiomers
Molecules A and B are mirror images, but they are not superimposable on one another!
It is impossible to align all five atoms in A and B.
A and B are both chiral .

Chirality & Enantiomers-1
Molecules A and B are mirror images, but they are not superimposable on one another! They are enantiomers
Enantiomerism is a relationship between two molecules! A molecule cannot be an enantiomer in isolation
Note the four different groups attached to the tetrahedral carbon atom .
A and B are enantiomers: mirror images that are not identical.

Identical Mirror Images
Many molecules are identical to their mirror images
To test, reflect the molecule through a mirror and try to superimpose the mirror image on the original
We can simply slide the mirror image on top of the original to see that CHClF 2 is achiral .

Section 4.2
THE CHIRALITY CENTER

The Chirality Center
A carbon bonded to four different groups w – z is called a chirality center
When w – z are all achiral groups and all different, C wxyz is a chiral compound
A chirality center can be used with care to identify a compound as chiral
Molecules with more than one chirality center may be achiral !

Chirality Centers in Rings
Note that any differences in ring groups make them distinct for the purposes of identifying chirality centers
The starred carbon is a chirality center. It is bound to four different groups: CH 3 , CH 2 , H, and O !
Carbon 4 is a chirality center. The chain including carbons 3 and 2 is different from the C5-C6 chain .

Section 4.3
SYMMETRY IN ACHIRAL STRUCTURES

Elements of Symmetry
Achiral molecules include planes of symmetry and/or inversion centers that leave the appearance of the molecule completely unchanged
Plane of symmetry: an internal reflection plane
Inversion center: a point through which all atoms are projected

A Shortcut for Identifying Chirality
Molecules that contain planes of symmetry (or inversion centers) are necessarily achiral
Hence, we can look for a plane of symmetry to identify a molecule as achiral
It is important to look hard when applying this method!
Generating the mirror image and trying to superimpose it on the original is the failsafe method

Section 4.4
OPTICAL ACTIVITY

Rotation of Plane-polarized Light
Chiral compounds containing an excess of one enantiomer over the other rotate the plane of plane-polarized light
Optical rotation can be measured using two polarizing filters, one of which rotates

Enantiomeric Mixtures
Racemic mixtures : Mixtures containing equal quantities of enantiomers . Racemic mixtures are optically inactive.
Homochiral or enantiopure : All the molecules in a mixture are of the same handedness
Enantiomeric excess : a mixture of enantiomers where one isomer is present in larger amounts
e.e. = (% Major enantiomer) − (% Minor enantiomer )

Specific Rotation & Enantiomeric Excess
Pure (+) enantiomer rotates light in one direction; pure ( − ) enantiomer rotates light to the same angle in the opposite direction
Optical rotation depends on the length ( l ) and concentration ( c ) of the sample; we can divide out these variables to obtain specific rotation ([ α ])
The specific rotation of a mixture of (+) and (−) enantiomers is linearly related to [a] for the pure enantiomer and the enantiomeric excess ( ee )

Section 4.5
ABSOLUTE AND RELATIVE CONFIGURATION

Absolute and Relative Configuration
The absolute configuration of a molecule is the full specification of the three-dimensional positions of its atoms
Absolute configuration ultimately must be confirmed by x-ray crystallography
However, the relative configurations of two related compounds can be deduced if conversion of one to the other does not involve chirality centers in the compounds
These compounds have the same relative configuration.

Relative Configuration & Optical Rotation
Compounds with the same relative configuration may have opposite signs of optical rotation!
In general, the sign of optical rotation cannot be deduced from structural information alone
